fragments.content: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by persistent patterns of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity interfering with functioning or development. Inattention symptoms include difficulty sustaining attention, careless mistakes, not listening, failing to follow through, disorganization, avoiding sustained mental effort, losing things, distractibility, forgetfulness. Hyperactivity-impulsivity includes fidgeting, leaving seat, running/climbing excessively, inability to play quietly, being 'on the go,' excessive talking, blurting answers, difficulty waiting turn, interrupting. Symptoms present before age 12, occur in ≥2 settings, and cause impairment. Lifetime prevalence ~5-7% in children, ~2-5% in adults. Pathophysiology involves dopamine/norepinephrine dysregulation in prefrontal-striatal circuits, high heritability (~70-80%).

First-line pharmacotherapy: stimulants (methylphenidate or amphetamine formulations) with robust evidence for efficacy (~70-80% response rate). Long-acting formulations preferred for adherence and reduced abuse potential. Non-stimulants: atomoxetine (SNRI), guanfacine/clonidine (alpha-2 agonists), viloxazine—used when stimulants ineffective/not tolerated or as adjuncts. Behavioral interventions: parent training (children), classroom management, organizational skills training, CBT (adults). Combination therapy often optimal. Monitoring includes growth (children), cardiovascular parameters, sleep, appetite, mood changes. Treatment often continues long-term given chronic nature.

DSM-5-TR ADHD criteria: ≥6 symptoms (≥5 for age ≥17) of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity persisting ≥6 months to degree inconsistent with developmental level. Several symptoms present before age 12. Present in ≥2 settings (home, school/work, social). Clear evidence of interference with functioning/quality of life. Not better explained by another mental disorder. Presentation specifiers: combined, predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive-impulsive. Severity specifiers: mild, moderate, severe. Partial remission specifier for those previously meeting full criteria. Common comorbidities: learning disorders, oppositional defiant disorder, anxiety, depression, substance use disorders.
